metadata.title: Fictional example: token cache consistency for profile picture generation

blocks.text: In this fictional example, stale avatar URLs were served after profile updates. A refactor centralizes token refresh and cache invalidation in the auth gateway.

items: Cache lookups could return expired profile URLs when token refresh fails silently.
Retry behavior duplicated across three call paths with inconsistent edge-case handling.
Tests only asserted success cases and missed token rotation rollback scenarios.

blocks.text: Example evidence map: src/auth/gateway.py, tests/test_gateway.py, and docs/cache.md in a hypothetical inspected branch. Replace these with verified paths from the real change.
Think of the cache as a signed notebook. Before this change, every reader touched it in a different way. Now all updates flow through one checkpoint before data is returned.

steps: Validate token age and metadata before cache read.
Resolve token only through the shared `TokenResolver` helper.
Persist refreshed token only once and return normalized payload.

blocks.label: Before (conceptual pattern)
blocks.code:
def get_avatar_url(user_id):
    # Multiple handlers, partial cleanup
    cached = CACHE.get(user_id)
    if cached and cached.valid:
        return cached.value
    token = refresh_token(user_id)
    CACHE[user_id] = token
    return token

before.text: Three routes checked token state independently and each one logged a different edge case.

after.text: A single resolver now owns state transitions and returns deterministic results for all callers.

blocks.label: After (conceptual pattern)
blocks.code:
def resolve_profile_avatar(user_id, *, now=utcnow):
    state = token_store.get(user_id)
    if state and not state.is_stale(now):
        return state.url

    refreshed = token_resolver.refresh(user_id)
    token_store.invalidate(user_id)
    token_store.upsert(user_id, refreshed)
    return refreshed.url

blocks.text: Keep this section in sync with diff line references in the verification notes section.

questions.prompt: Why was a shared resolver introduced in the refactor?
options: To deduplicate token refresh logic and guarantee identical rollback behavior across callers.
To speed up startup time in every route.
To remove cache usage entirely.
To make token generation asynchronous by default.
questions.explanation: The resolver aligns shared edge cases and prevents stale writes.

questions.prompt: What was the highest-priority bug symptom described in the scenario?
options: Excessive CPU usage on unrelated endpoints.
Stale avatar URLs after profile updates.
A login loop for all users.
A schema migration failure in production.
questions.explanation: The stated incident focused on stale values being returned.

questions.prompt: Which section is the best place to place the caller impact note?
options: Only in the final section summary.
Within the code section, next to the new helper contract.
Inside the quiz question explanations.
In an external system design diagram only.
questions.explanation: Caller impact is part of implementation behavior, not quiz text.

questions.prompt: What does deterministic output help with for this skill?
options: It avoids randomness in diff inspection.
It removes the need for metadata.
It guarantees reproducible quiz shuffling for review and testing.
It encrypts local HTML artifacts automatically.
questions.explanation: Reproducibility makes review and artifact drift tests stable.

questions.prompt: Which output constraint is required by the renderer?
options: Must be a single offline, self-contained HTML file.
Must only include remote scripts and styles for richer rendering.
Must push directly to cloud storage without user review.
Must include inline click handlers for immediate interaction.
Must execute remote API calls when opened.
questions.explanation: Artifacts are local, standalone, and offline by contract.
